stem: A 41-year-old woman is referred by her general practitioner to a community psychiatry clinic after 18 months of persistent abdominal pain and fatigue. She has had two normal colonoscopies, a normal gastroscopy, normal abdominal CT, normal coeliac serology, and a normal pelvic ultrasound across this period. She has consulted 11 different clinicians, frequently checks symptom-tracking apps and online forums for several hours each day, and reports that the uncertainty about what is wrong is so distressing that she has reduced her part-time bookkeeping work to one day per week. She describes the pain as a constant 6 out of 10, worsening with stress, and is convinced something serious has been missed. She denies low mood pervading her day, denies anhedonia, sleeps 6 hours nightly with early waking only when pain is severe, and her K-10 is 24. She uses paracetamol most days and has no other regular medications. She has never had clear hypomanic, psychotic, or panic episodes.

data_table.Examination: Anxious, preoccupied with bodily focus, organised thought, no psychosis. Mild epigastric tenderness, otherwise unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: K-10 24. FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH, coeliac serology, faecal calprotectin all within reference. Two prior colonoscopies and a gastroscopy normal.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate DSM-5 classification for this presentation?

options.text: Illness anxiety disorder with care-seeking behaviour
options.rationale: Illness anxiety disorder requires preoccupation with HAVING or acquiring a serious illness with minimal or absent somatic symptoms; her dominant feature is the symptom (pain) itself, not feared disease (DSM-5, RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Somatic symptom disorder with persistent course
options.rationale: DSM-5 SSD requires one or more distressing somatic symptoms PLUS disproportionate or persistent thoughts, anxiety, or behaviours about them, for more than 6 months. Her persistent pain, excessive health-related time use, functional decline, and disproportionate concern across 18 months meet criteria; persistent course is specified at >6 months of severe symptoms (DSM-5; RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Generalised anxiety disorder with somatic predominance
options.rationale: GAD requires excessive worry across multiple domains for >=6 months with at least three of restlessness, fatigue, concentration, irritability, muscle tension, or sleep disturbance; her worry is narrowly focused on a single bodily symptom, which is the SSD pattern (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Functional neurological disorder, mixed sensory type
options.rationale: FND requires one or more altered voluntary motor or sensory functions with positive incompatibility signs (e.g., Hoover's, tremor entrainment); abdominal pain alone without neurological symptoms does not satisfy FND (RANZCP; FND Society guidance).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Factitious disorder imposed on self
options.rationale: Factitious disorder requires intentional falsification or induction of signs or symptoms with deceptive intent in the absence of obvious external reward; there is no evidence of fabrication here, only genuine distress (DSM-5).

explanation.summary: Somatic symptom disorder hinges on the response to symptoms (disproportionate thoughts, anxiety, time, behaviours) rather than whether symptoms are medically explained. DSM-5 moved away from 'medically unexplained' framing toward affirming the symptom and addressing the disproportionate cognitive-behavioural reaction (RANZCP; DSM-5).
key_points: SSD = >=1 distressing somatic symptom + disproportionate thoughts/anxiety/behaviours + >6 months
Illness anxiety disorder centres on feared disease with little or no somatic symptom
FND requires positive neurological incompatibility signs
Factitious disorder requires deceptive intent without external incentive
explanation.pearls: Do not require 'unexplained' symptoms for SSD - genuine medical conditions can coexist (RANZCP).

sources.label: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

stem: A 29-year-old graphic designer presents to the emergency department with sudden onset right leg weakness that began 3 days ago after a minor work argument. She has no headache, no visual change, no bowel or bladder symptoms, and no sensory level. She walks with a dragging right leg that appears to swing in a circumduction not typical of pyramidal hemiparesis. Power in the right hip extensors is 2/5 when tested in isolation, but when she is asked to flex the left hip against resistance while lying supine, you feel strong downward pressure under her right heel. Right knee and ankle reflexes are normal and symmetric, plantar response is flexor, sensation is reported as patchy with a non-anatomical boundary at mid-thigh. Cranial nerves are intact. MRI brain and whole spine completed yesterday are normal. FBC, UEC, glucose, TSH, B12 and HIV serology are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 76, BP 118/72, RR 14, SpO2 99% on air, afebrile.
data_table.Examination: Hoover's sign positive on the right (involuntary hip extension during contralateral hip flexion against resistance). Reflexes symmetric, plantars flexor, non-anatomical sensory boundary.
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain and spine normal. FBC, UEC, glucose, TSH, B12, HIV serology normal.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat MRI brain with stroke protocol and start dual antiplatelet therapy
options.rationale: Stroke is unlikely with normal MRI, symmetric reflexes, flexor plantar, and a positive Hoover's sign indicating functional weakness; antiplatelets in this setting carry bleeding risk without diagnostic justification (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).

options.text: Admit for high-dose IV methylprednisolone empirically for suspected multiple sclerosis
options.rationale: MS is excluded by normal MRI and the positive functional sign; steroids carry harm and would entrench illness behaviour (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).

options.text: Tell the patient the scan is normal and there is nothing wrong, with GP follow up in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Telling FND patients 'nothing is wrong' worsens distress, increases relapse, and is associated with poorer outcomes; FND is a positive diagnosis that must be communicated as real and treatable (FND Society 2021).

options.text: Explain the positive Hoover's sign as a rule-in diagnosis of functional neurological disorder and refer for FND-specific physiotherapy
options.rationale: FND is a positive diagnosis based on incompatibility signs (Hoover's, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness with normal MRI and reflexes). The first therapeutic step is a clear, non-stigmatising explanation that the diagnosis is made BY the positive sign, framed as a brain functioning problem that is treatable, followed by referral to FND-trained physiotherapy which is the strongest evidence-based intervention (RANZCP; FND Society Consensus; NICE NG217 stroke excluded).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Commence quetiapine 25 mg nocte for somatisation and arrange psychiatric admission
options.rationale: Antipsychotics are not first-line for FND and inpatient psychiatric admission is rarely indicated; physiotherapy is the evidence-based primary treatment, with psychological therapies as adjunct (RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

explanation.summary: Functional neurological disorder is a positive diagnosis. Hoover's sign (involuntary recovery of hip extension during contralateral hip flexion) is the bedside test that rules in functional leg weakness. Treatment hinges on a clear non-blaming explanation plus FND-specific physiotherapy (FND Society 2021).
key_points: FND positive signs: Hoover's, hip abductor sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness with normal reflexes
Communicate diagnosis as real, common, and treatable; avoid 'nothing wrong'
FND-specific physiotherapy has the strongest evidence base
Psychological therapy is adjunctive, not primary
explanation.pearls: If MRI is normal AND a positive sign is present, you have rule-in evidence - say so explicitly to the patient (FND Society).

sources.label: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

subtopic_detail: Illness anxiety disorder vs somatic symptom disorder
stem: A 38-year-old IT consultant attends his general practitioner for the fourth time in 8 weeks requesting another full blood count and tumour marker panel. He is convinced he has pancreatic cancer despite a normal abdominal CT, normal CA 19-9, normal LFTs, and normal pancreatic enzymes obtained 3 weeks ago. He has minor occasional epigastric flutter and mild fatigue but no weight loss, no jaundice, no night sweats, and no anorexia. He spends 3 to 4 hours daily on patient forums and oncology websites, has read three textbooks on pancreatic cancer staging, and asks colleagues to feel his abdomen for masses. His K-10 is 22. He acknowledges every reassurance gives him relief for around 24 hours before doubt returns. There is no family history of pancreatic cancer. He has no other regular medications. He sleeps 6 hours and his weight is stable. His functional decline at work is described as moderate.

data_table.Examination: Anxious, pressured speech around health topics, no thought disorder. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no masses.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, lipase, CA 19-9 all within reference. Abdominal CT 3 weeks ago: normal.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate DSM-5 diagnosis?

options.text: Somatic symptom disorder with predominant pain
options.rationale: SSD requires one or more distressing somatic symptoms as the focus; his somatic symptoms are minor and the dominant feature is fear of having pancreatic cancer rather than distress from a symptom (DSM-5).

options.text: Generalised anxiety disorder, severe
options.rationale: GAD requires worry across multiple domains for >=6 months; his worry is narrowly focused on a single feared disease, which is the illness anxiety pattern (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Illness anxiety disorder, care-seeking type
options.rationale: DSM-5 illness anxiety disorder: preoccupation with having or acquiring a serious illness; somatic symptoms are absent or mild; high health-related anxiety; excessive health-related behaviours OR maladaptive avoidance; duration >=6 months; not better explained by another disorder. Repeated investigations, reassurance-seeking, and forum use mark the care-seeking type (DSM-5; RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Obsessive-compulsive disorder with contamination obsessions
options.rationale: OCD requires obsessions and/or compulsions consuming >1 hour daily; his behaviours are health-focused reassurance-seeking, not classical OCD obsessions or compulsive rituals such as washing or checking unrelated to health (DSM-5).

options.text: Delusional disorder, somatic type
options.rationale: Delusional disorder somatic type requires fixed false beliefs of bodily disease held with delusional intensity; he accepts reassurance partially and his belief fluctuates, indicating anxiety rather than delusion (RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

explanation.summary: Illness anxiety disorder is dominated by fear of having or acquiring a serious illness with minimal somatic symptoms; SSD is dominated by distressing somatic symptoms themselves. Discriminator: 'is the patient distressed by the symptom or by what the symptom means?' (DSM-5).
key_points: IAD: feared disease + minimal somatic symptoms + >6 months + health-related behaviours
Care-seeking type repeatedly investigates; care-avoidant type avoids medical contact
Reassurance provides only short-term relief, a hallmark feature
Differentiate from delusional disorder by reality testing
explanation.pearls: If reassurance helps for 24 hours then doubt returns, think illness anxiety, not delusion (RANZCP).

sources.label: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

stem: A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department for the 7th time in 8 months with recurrent hypoglycaemia. Each admission has shown plasma glucose 2.1 to 2.6 mmol/L with markedly elevated insulin and SUPPRESSED C-peptide. He has no diabetes, no insulinoma on prior CT and MRI, and no sulphonylurea detected on urine screen. He is a former endocrinology nurse, currently unemployed, and lives alone. He denies injecting insulin and becomes irritable when asked. There is no pending litigation, no Centrelink claim under review, no workers compensation case, and no current criminal charge or active forensic process. Staff have observed him discarding small objects in the sharps bin after symptom onset. Each admission has involved invasive investigations and extended length of stay. His K-10 is 30 and he reports profound loneliness.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, BP 128/82, RR 16, BSL 2.3 mmol/L on arrival.
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, oriented, no focal neurology.
data_table.Investigations: Plasma glucose 2.3 mmol/L, insulin 480 pmol/L (markedly raised), C-peptide 90 pmol/L (suppressed), sulphonylurea screen negative. CT and MRI pancreas previously normal.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate DSM-5 classification of his behaviour?

options.text: Malingering for secondary financial gain
options.rationale: Malingering is intentional production of symptoms for an EXTERNAL incentive (avoiding work, military duty, criminal prosecution, securing benefits, obtaining drugs). The stem explicitly excludes pending litigation, Centrelink review, workers compensation, criminal proceedings, and drug-seeking - so external incentive is absent (DSM-5).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Factitious disorder imposed on self
options.rationale: Factitious disorder requires intentional falsification or induction of signs/symptoms WITH deceptive intent, presentation of self as ill, AND absence of obvious external reward. Suppressed C-peptide with elevated insulin and negative sulphonylurea screen confirms exogenous insulin administration; secretive discarding of objects and former medical employment supports falsification; no external incentive is identifiable. The motive is the sick role itself (DSM-5; RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Somatic symptom disorder with predominant pain
options.rationale: SSD requires genuine distressing somatic symptoms with disproportionate response WITHOUT intentional falsification; suppressed C-peptide proves exogenous insulin, which is induction not genuine pathology (DSM-5).

options.text: Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder)
options.rationale: Conversion disorder requires altered voluntary motor/sensory function inconsistent with recognised neurological disease but is NOT intentionally produced; the lab pattern here proves intentional induction (DSM-5).

options.text: Delusional disorder, somatic type
options.rationale: Delusional disorder requires fixed false belief; he is intentionally inducing pathology, not holding a fixed belief about disease (RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

explanation.summary: Factitious disorder vs malingering hinges on motivation: factitious is intentional symptom production motivated by the sick role with NO external incentive; malingering is intentional symptom production for external incentive (benefits, avoidance, drugs). Suppressed C-peptide with elevated insulin is a textbook signature of surreptitious insulin use (DSM-5; AMH).
key_points: Factitious disorder: intentional + deceptive + no external incentive (sick-role motive)
Malingering: intentional + deceptive + external incentive
Suppressed C-peptide + high insulin + negative sulphonylurea = exogenous insulin
Approach: avoid confrontation, address underlying psychological need, consult-liaison referral
explanation.pearls: Document objective evidence of induction (lab pattern, hidden items) before formulating - confrontation typically escalates the behaviour (RANZCP).

sources.label: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

stem: A 52-year-old woman is referred to community mental health by her general practitioner for a 4-month history of fatigue, low mood, weight gain of 5 kg, constipation, cold intolerance, and reduced concentration. She has stopped her weekly bushwalking group and finds her brain feels foggy. Her sister has Hashimoto thyroiditis and her mother had pernicious anaemia. She has no prior psychiatric history, no suicidal ideation, no psychotic symptoms, and her affect is flat with mildly slowed thought tempo. She is not pregnant. She takes no regular medications and drinks 4 standard drinks weekly. Her general practitioner started sertraline 50 mg daily 6 weeks ago without baseline bloods, with no improvement, and has now sent her with K-10 of 28. On examination her skin is dry, reflexes show delayed relaxation phase, and there is a small diffuse non-tender goitre. HR is 52.

data_table.Examination: Slow speech, flat affect, dry skin, delayed reflex relaxation, small diffuse goitre.
data_table.Investigations: Not yet performed by mental health team.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next investigation before adjusting psychotropic therapy?

options.text: Switch sertraline to mirtazapine 30 mg nocte and review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Switching antidepressants without first excluding a medical mimic risks treating thyroid disease as treatment-resistant depression; AMH and RANZCP both mandate baseline TSH before labelling depression treatment-resistant (RANZCP; AMH).

options.text: Dexamethasone suppression test for Cushing syndrome
options.rationale: Cushing screening is reserved for features suggestive of cortisol excess (central adiposity, striae, proximal myopathy, hypertension, hyperglycaemia); this presentation suggests hypothyroidism not hypercortisolism (Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).

options.text: Brain MRI to exclude frontal lobe lesion
options.rationale: MRI is not first-line in a presentation with clear thyroid-pointing physical findings and no focal neurology; pre-test probability of structural lesion is low compared to autoimmune thyroid disease (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).

options.text: TSH, free T4 and thyroid peroxidase antibodies
options.rationale: Clinical features (bradycardia, delayed reflex relaxation, goitre, cold intolerance, weight gain, family history of autoimmune thyroid disease) point to primary hypothyroidism, most likely Hashimoto. TSH plus free T4 is the screening pair; TPO antibodies confirm autoimmune aetiology and guide monitoring of subclinical disease. RANZCP and Therapeutic Guidelines require excluding thyroid disease before labelling depression as primary (RANZCP; eTG).

options.text: Empirical levothyroxine 50 microgram daily without bloods
options.rationale: Australian Medicines Handbook requires confirmed biochemical hypothyroidism before levothyroxine commencement to avoid iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis; empirical treatment is not standard (AMH).

explanation.summary: Before labelling a depressive presentation as primary, screen for medical mimics with TSH at minimum. Bradycardia, delayed reflex relaxation, goitre, and an autoimmune family history make hypothyroidism a leading mimic and a treatable cause of mood and cognitive symptoms (RANZCP; eTG).
key_points: Baseline screen for mood presentations: TSH, FBC, UEC, LFTs, B12/folate, glucose, urine drug screen if indicated
Hypothyroid mimic features: bradycardia, delayed reflex relaxation, dry skin, goitre, cold intolerance
TPO antibodies confirm Hashimoto
Empirical levothyroxine without bloods risks thyrotoxicosis
explanation.pearls: Treatment-resistant depression with no baseline TSH is a diagnostic red flag, not a pharmacological problem (RANZCP).

stem: A 22-year-old previously well university student is admitted to a psychiatric inpatient unit with a 3-week history of insomnia, paranoid delusions, and disorganised behaviour. Over 48 hours her presentation has changed: she now has orofacial dyskinesias, brief episodes of unresponsiveness with eyelid twitching, autonomic instability with episodes of HR 140 then HR 48, BP swings between 165/95 and 95/55, and a low-grade fever of 38.1. She has had no recent antipsychotic doses (admitted before any were given). She has no recent illicit substance use on urine drug screen. Her CK is 320 U/L. There is no neck stiffness. Her family report a brief flu-like illness 6 weeks ago. CT head is normal. Lumbar puncture shows mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (35 cells/microL, predominantly lymphocytes) with normal glucose and mildly raised protein.

data_table.Vital signs: Fluctuating HR 48-140, BP 95/55 to 165/95, T 38.1, RR variable.
data_table.Examination: Orofacial dyskinesias, intermittent unresponsiveness with eyelid twitching, no neck stiffness, no focal deficit.
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen negative. CK 320 U/L. CT head normal. CSF: WCC 35 (lymphocytic), protein 0.65 g/L, glucose 3.2 mmol/L (paired plasma 5.0).

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Serum and CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibodies plus MRI brain with contrast
options.rationale: Sub-acute psychiatric symptoms in a young woman PLUS movement disorder (orofacial dyskinesias) PLUS autonomic instability PLUS seizure-like episodes PLUS lymphocytic CSF pleocytosis is the textbook pentad of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. Serum and CSF antibody testing PLUS MRI is the diagnostic pathway; early immunotherapy improves outcome (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology; RANZCP medical mimics).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Increase olanzapine to 20 mg daily and add lorazepam for agitation
options.rationale: Treating as primary psychosis without excluding autoimmune encephalitis risks delayed immunotherapy, worse outcomes, and antipsychotic-induced NMS-like deterioration in encephalitis patients (RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Empirical IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg 8-hourly for HSV encephalitis only
options.rationale: Aciclovir is appropriate empirically while encephalitis is being worked up, but it is not the most informative SINGLE next test, and HSV alone does not explain the orofacial dyskinesias and autonomic storm; antibody testing plus MRI is the diagnostic pathway (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: EEG plus measurement of serum creatine kinase trend for NMS
options.rationale: NMS is unlikely as she has had no antipsychotic exposure (admitted before any dose) and CK is only mildly elevated; EEG can support encephalitis but is not the disease-specific test (RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Whole-body PET CT to exclude paraneoplastic origin only
options.rationale: Paraneoplastic workup (e.g., ovarian teratoma in anti-NMDAR) follows confirmation of the antibody syndrome; the immediate next test is antibody plus MRI (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).

explanation.summary: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis is the highest-yield psychiatric mimic to recognise in young women presenting with sub-acute psychosis plus movement disorder, autonomic instability, seizures, and lymphocytic CSF. Early antibody testing and immunotherapy improve outcomes; first-pass treatment as primary psychosis worsens prognosis (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).
key_points: Pentad: psychiatric symptoms, movement disorder, seizures, autonomic instability, reduced consciousness
CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, mildly raised protein
Test BOTH serum and CSF NMDAR antibodies (CSF is more sensitive)
Look for ovarian teratoma once antibody confirmed
Antipsychotics can paradoxically worsen presentation (NMS-like)
explanation.pearls: If a young woman with new psychosis has orofacial dyskinesias before any antipsychotic exposure, think NMDAR encephalitis (Lancet Neurology consensus).

sources.label: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

stem: An 84-year-old woman with Alzheimer disease (baseline MoCA 16/30, lives at home with daily community nursing) is admitted with a community-acquired pneumonia and treated with IV ceftriaxone plus oral azithromycin. On day 3 of admission her family report she is markedly different from baseline: she is drowsy in the morning, agitated and pulling at her cannula by evening, sees small children in the curtains, and cannot identify where she is. Her usual mild forgetfulness has been replaced with fluctuating attention - she cannot maintain a conversation for more than 30 seconds. Her oxygen saturation is 95% on 2 L. She has not opened her bowels in 4 days. Urinalysis shows mild leucocytes only. Recent additions to her chart include oxycodone 5 mg 4-hourly PRN (taken regularly), lorazepam 1 mg PRN at night, and metoclopramide. Her CXR shows improving consolidation.

data_table.Examination: Fluctuating attention, inattentive on months-of-year backwards, visual hallucinations, distractible.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, CRP improving. Glucose 6.4 mmol/L. Urinalysis mild leukocytes only. CXR improving. ECG normal.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Add scheduled risperidone 1 mg BD and continue oxycodone, lorazepam, and metoclopramide
options.rationale: Antipsychotics are not first-line for delirium; they are reserved for severe agitation with risk of harm at the lowest effective dose. Continuing deliriogenic medications is the proximal cause and must be addressed first (RANZCP delirium; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).
source_refs: Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care - Delirium Clinical Care Standard

options.text: Start donepezil 5 mg daily for worsening Alzheimer disease and discharge home
options.rationale: Cholinesterase inhibitors are NOT indicated for acute delirium; the acute change reflects superimposed delirium not progression of dementia (Australian Medicines Handbook).

options.text: Transfer to closed psychiatric ward under involuntary order for management of psychosis
options.rationale: Delirium is a medical syndrome managed on the medical ward; transfer to psychiatric care delays treatment of the underlying medical cause (Delirium Clinical Care Standard).
source_refs: Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care - Delirium Clinical Care Standard

options.text: Minimise deliriogenic drugs, address constipation, optimise non-pharmacological measures, treat UTI only if supported
options.rationale: The Australian Commission Delirium Clinical Care Standard mandates a structured non-pharmacological bundle: identify and treat reversible precipitants (medications, constipation, pain, hypoxia, infection, dehydration, sleep-wake disruption), reorientation, hearing/vision aids, mobilisation, hydration, family presence. Asymptomatic bacteriuria should NOT be treated with antibiotics in older adults as it does not cause delirium and antibiotic exposure worsens it (Delirium Clinical Care Standard; eTG Antibiotic).
source_refs: Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care - Delirium Clinical Care Standard

options.text: Commence intravenous ciprofloxacin empirically for presumed urinary tract infection based on urinalysis
options.rationale: Mild leucocytes on urinalysis in an older adult without urinary symptoms represents asymptomatic bacteriuria and does not warrant antibiotics; treatment risks Clostridioides difficile and further delirium (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: Hospital delirium superimposed on dementia is identified by acute change from baseline, fluctuating course, inattention, and altered cognition. The Australian Delirium Clinical Care Standard prioritises identifying and reversing precipitants over pharmacological sedation (Australian Commission).
key_points: DSM-5 delirium: acute change, fluctuation, inattention, additional cognitive disturbance, not better explained by dementia alone
First step: address deliriogenic medications, constipation, pain, hypoxia, hydration, sleep
Antipsychotics only for severe agitation with safety risk, lowest effective dose, short duration
Avoid treating asymptomatic bacteriuria in older adults
explanation.pearls: Opioids, benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, and metoclopramide are the highest-yield culprits to deprescribe (eTG).

sources.label: Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care - Delirium Clinical Care Standard

stem: A 52-year-old man with recurrent major depressive disorder has had no adequate response to sertraline 200 mg daily for 10 weeks followed by venlafaxine titrated to 225 mg daily for 8 weeks. Adherence is confirmed by pharmacy records and partner observation. His PHQ-9 has fallen from 22 to 19 only. There has been no significant intolerance. He has no bipolar history and no psychotic features. He is not catatonic, not refusing food, has no immediate suicide plan, and his K-10 today is 30. He sleeps 5 hours and has lost 3 kg over 8 weeks. His baseline bloods today are FBC normal, UEC normal (eGFR 92), TSH 1.8, fasting glucose 5.2, LFTs normal, ECG QTc 410 ms. He is a non-smoker, drinks 4 standard drinks weekly, and has no chronic medical illness. RANZCP defines treatment-resistant depression as failure of two adequate antidepressant trials at therapeutic dose and duration.

data_table.Examination: Low mood, congruent affect, no psychosis, no catatonia.
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 19. FBC, UEC (eGFR 92), TSH 1.8, fasting glucose 5.2, LFTs all within reference. ECG QTc 410 ms.
data_table.Medications: Venlafaxine 225 mg daily (current), sertraline 200 mg daily for 10 weeks previously.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Add lithium augmentation to trough 0.4-0.8 mmol/L with renal and thyroid monitoring
options.rationale: RANZCP CPG 2020 for treatment-resistant depression endorses lithium augmentation as a first-line augmentation strategy with the strongest evidence base, target trough level 0.4 to 0.8 mmol/L for augmentation (lower than mania prophylaxis target). His normal eGFR, normal TSH, and absence of contraindications support this choice. Baseline U+E, TSH, calcium and ECG plus 12-week-interval monitoring are required (RANZCP; AMH).

options.text: Switch venlafaxine to fluoxetine 20 mg daily as monotherapy without any augmentation
options.rationale: Switching to a third SSRI as monotherapy after two failed trials is lower-yield than augmentation in confirmed TRD; switching may be appropriate after one failure with intolerance, not after two adequate trials with partial response (RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Add bupropion 150 mg daily as monoamine combination augmentation strategy
options.rationale: Bupropion combination has weaker evidence in TRD than lithium or atypical augmentation; it is not first-line in RANZCP TRD guidance (RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Add diazepam 5 mg BD as anxiolytic augmentation for six weeks duration
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines have no role in TRD augmentation, risk dependence, and may worsen depressive symptoms; they are not endorsed by RANZCP or eTG for this indication (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Refer urgently for ECT as first-line treatment for resistant depressive illness
options.rationale: ECT is indicated when rapid response is required (catatonia, refusal of food/fluid, high suicide risk, severe psychotic depression) or after multiple pharmacological strategies have failed; he is not in any of those categories so pharmacological augmentation is offered first (RANZCP CPG).

explanation.summary: RANZCP defines TRD as failure of two adequate antidepressant trials. Lithium augmentation (target 0.4-0.8 mmol/L) and atypical antipsychotic augmentation (quetiapine or aripiprazole) are first-line; mirtazapine combination is reasonable; ECT is reserved for severe or rapid-response indications (RANZCP CPG 2020).
key_points: TRD = inadequate response to 2 adequate trials at therapeutic dose and duration
Lithium augmentation target 0.4-0.8 mmol/L (lower than mood stabilisation)
Baseline U+E, TSH, calcium, ECG before lithium; recheck level after 5-7 days
ECT indicated for catatonia, food refusal, high suicide risk, psychotic depression
Avoid benzodiazepines as augmentation
explanation.pearls: Lithium augmentation works within 2-4 weeks - re-evaluate at week 4, not week 12 (RANZCP).

stem: A 47-year-old woman with MDD has been on fluoxetine 40 mg daily for 9 months with partial response (PHQ-9 only fallen from 21 to 16) and persistent fatigue, sexual dysfunction, and weight gain. Her psychiatrist plans to switch her to phenelzine, a non-selective irreversible monoamine oxidase inhibitor, given her atypical features. She has no significant medical illness, no concurrent serotonergic medications, no St John's Wort, no tramadol, no triptans, and is not pregnant. Her renal and hepatic function are normal. The patient asks how the switch will be done safely. She has no immediate suicide risk, lives with her supportive partner, and is willing to attend weekly review during the transition.

data_table.Examination: Mild psychomotor slowing, otherwise unremarkable, no autonomic instability.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH all within reference. ECG normal.
data_table.Medications: Fluoxetine 40 mg daily for 9 months, no other serotonergic agents.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate switching strategy?

options.text: Direct switch: stop fluoxetine today and commence phenelzine 15 mg TDS tomorrow
options.rationale: Direct switch from any SSRI to an MAOI risks fatal serotonin syndrome and is contraindicated; washout is mandatory (AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Cross-taper: reduce fluoxetine while simultaneously titrating phenelzine over 2 weeks
options.rationale: Cross-tapering between SSRI and MAOI is contraindicated because of overlapping serotonergic activity; AMH and eTG specifically require a complete washout (AMH).

options.text: Stop fluoxetine and wait 5 weeks before commencing phenelzine for adequate norfluoxetine washout
options.rationale: Switching FROM fluoxetine TO an MAOI requires at least 5 weeks washout because of fluoxetine's active metabolite norfluoxetine (half-life 7-15 days). All other SSRIs/SNRIs require minimum 2 weeks washout before an MAOI; switching FROM an MAOI TO another antidepressant requires 2 weeks for the MAO enzyme to regenerate. These are absolute rules in AMH and Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic.

options.text: Stop fluoxetine and wait 5 days before starting phenelzine
options.rationale: Five days is insufficient given fluoxetine plus norfluoxetine half-life; this would carry significant serotonin syndrome risk (AMH).

options.text: Continue fluoxetine and add low-dose phenelzine 7.5 mg daily as augmentation
options.rationale: Combining an SSRI with an MAOI is contraindicated and a serotonin syndrome death risk; never coadminister (AMH).

explanation.summary: Switching from fluoxetine to a non-selective irreversible MAOI requires a 5-week washout because norfluoxetine has a half-life of 7-15 days. Other SSRI/SNRI to MAOI switches require 2 weeks; MAOI to another antidepressant requires 2 weeks for enzyme regeneration (AMH; eTG).
key_points: Fluoxetine to MAOI: 5-week washout (norfluoxetine half-life)
Other SSRI/SNRI to MAOI: 2-week washout
MAOI to another antidepressant: 2-week washout (enzyme regeneration)
Cross-taper and direct switch SSRI to MAOI are contraindicated
Watch for serotonergic drug interactions: tramadol, triptans, linezolid, St John's Wort, pethidine
explanation.pearls: Fluoxetine's washout rules dominate any switching plan involving an MAOI (AMH).

subtopic_detail: Serotonin syndrome vs NMS vs anticholinergic discrimination
stem: A 28-year-old man is brought to ED by ambulance 6 hours after his community mental health team added tramadol 100 mg QID for back pain. He was already on escitalopram 20 mg daily and recently started linezolid for a soft tissue infection. He is agitated, with shivering, sweating, and intermittent myoclonus that is most prominent in the LOWER LIMBS. His pupils are dilated (6 mm) and reactive. He has hyperreflexia with inducible clonus, most marked at the ankles, and ocular clonus is also present. His skin is moist (not dry). HR 128, BP 168/92, RR 22, T 39.4. He is alert but agitated. He has no recent antipsychotic exposure. CK is 480 U/L.

data_table.Examination: Agitation, diaphoresis, dilated reactive pupils, lower-limb-predominant clonus, hyperreflexia, ocular clonus, moist skin.
data_table.Investigations: CK 480 U/L, FBC, UEC, glucose normal. Paracetamol level negative.
data_table.Medications: Escitalopram 20 mg daily, tramadol 100 mg QID (started today), linezolid for soft tissue infection.

lead_in: Which is the single most likely toxidrome?

options.text: Anticholinergic toxidrome from antimuscarinic overdose
options.rationale: Anticholinergic toxidrome shows DRY skin, dry mucous membranes, urinary retention, ileus, dilated pupils, and confusion without clonus or hyperreflexia; his moist skin and clonus exclude this (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

options.text: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome from antipsychotic dopamine blockade
options.rationale: NMS features 'lead-pipe' rigidity, HYPOreflexia or normal reflexes, hyperthermia, autonomic instability, markedly raised CK (often >1000), and develops over days; clonus and hyperreflexia point away from NMS, and he has had no antipsychotic exposure (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Serotonin syndrome from triple serotonergic combination
options.rationale: Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria require a serotonergic agent PLUS one of: spontaneous clonus; inducible clonus + agitation or diaphoresis; ocular clonus + agitation or diaphoresis; tremor + hyperreflexia; or hypertonia + temperature >38 with ocular or inducible clonus. He has multiple positive criteria. Onset is rapid (hours), and the pattern of LOWER-limb-predominant clonus, hyperreflexia, and ocular clonus is pathognomonic. Tramadol (serotonin reuptake inhibitor) plus linezolid (MAOI activity) plus escitalopram is a textbook triple-hit (Hunter criteria; eTG Toxicology).

options.text: Sympathomimetic toxidrome from stimulant intoxication
options.rationale: Stimulant toxidrome may cause hyperthermia, hypertension, and agitation but does NOT cause lower-limb-predominant clonus or ocular clonus; the medication exposure pattern and clonus distribution favour serotonin syndrome (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

options.text: Malignant hyperthermia from volatile anaesthetic exposure
options.rationale: Malignant hyperthermia requires exposure to volatile anaesthetic or suxamethonium with masseter rigidity, rising end-tidal CO2, and acidosis; this is a non-anaesthetic setting (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

explanation.summary: Three discriminators separate serotonin syndrome, NMS, and anticholinergic toxidrome: TIMING (SS hours; NMS days; anticholinergic hours), TONE (SS hyperreflexia + clonus; NMS lead-pipe rigidity + hyporeflexia; anticholinergic flaccid), and SKIN (SS and NMS sweaty; anticholinergic dry). Clonus, especially lower-limb and ocular, is the SS fingerprint (Hunter criteria; eTG).
key_points: SS: hours to onset, hyperreflexia, clonus (esp. lower limb + ocular), moist skin
NMS: days to onset, lead-pipe rigidity, hyporeflexia, moist skin, very high CK
Anticholinergic: hours to onset, dry skin/mucosae, mydriasis, urinary retention, NO clonus
Hunter criteria for SS require serotonergic exposure + one specific neuromuscular pattern
Treatment: stop all serotonergic drugs, cooling, IV benzodiazepines, cyproheptadine in severe cases
explanation.pearls: Linezolid is a hidden MAOI - combining with any serotonergic agent is high-risk (AMH).

stem: A 56-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder maintained on lithium carbonate 750 mg daily (trough level 0.7 mmol/L 6 weeks ago) presents to ED with 3 days of coarse tremor, ataxia, drowsiness, and vomiting. Over the past 2 weeks she has been treated for hypertension with newly added perindopril, has been taking ibuprofen 400 mg TDS for shoulder pain, and during a recent heatwave she has had reduced oral intake with no fluid replacement. She has lost 2 kg in 10 days. Her usual sertraline 50 mg daily has been unchanged. She is alert but confused, with hyperreflexia, coarse tremor, and gait ataxia. There is no clonus and no fever. Her ECG shows T-wave flattening with QTc 460 ms. Her current lithium level is 2.1 mmol/L. Creatinine has risen from baseline 75 to 145 micromol/L (eGFR fallen from 78 to 38).

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88, BP 142/86, RR 16, T 36.8, mild dehydration.
data_table.Examination: Confused, coarse tremor, gait ataxia, hyperreflexia, no clonus, no rigidity, no fever.
data_table.Investigations: Lithium 2.1 mmol/L, creatinine 145 (baseline 75), eGFR 38 (baseline 78), Na 138, K 4.2, ECG QTc 460 ms with T-wave flattening.
data_table.Medications: Lithium 750 mg daily, sertraline 50 mg daily, perindopril (new 2 weeks), ibuprofen 400 mg TDS (recent), no diuretics.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue lithium, give 1 L IV normal saline, recheck level in 24 hours
options.rationale: Lithium level 2.1 mmol/L with neurological features is moderate-to-severe toxicity; continuing lithium risks progression to seizures, coma, and irreversible nephrogenic diabetes insipidus or cerebellar damage. Lithium must be ceased immediately (AMH; eTG Psychotropic).

options.text: Continue lithium, withhold ACE inhibitor and ibuprofen, recheck level next week
options.rationale: Acute toxicity with neurological features and AKI requires immediate cessation and inpatient management; delaying re-check by a week risks fatal progression (eTG Psychotropic).

options.text: Give 50 g oral activated charcoal to bind unabsorbed lithium acutely
options.rationale: Activated charcoal does NOT bind lithium (an ion); it is ineffective in lithium toxicity (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

options.text: Cease lithium and give IV frusemide 40 mg to enhance lithium excretion
options.rationale: Loop diuretics WORSEN lithium toxicity by reducing volume and increasing proximal tubular lithium reabsorption; volume repletion with isotonic saline is the correct measure (AMH; eTG Toxicology).

options.text: Cease lithium, withdraw NSAID and ACE inhibitor, IV crystalloid, consult toxicology for haemodialysis
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology and AMH require: cease lithium immediately, withdraw precipitants (NSAID, ACE inhibitor, dehydration are the classic three), give IV crystalloid to restore intravascular volume, and consider haemodialysis. Australian thresholds for dialysis: level >4.0 mmol/L irrespective of symptoms, OR level 2.5-4.0 with significant neurotoxicity (confusion, seizures, decreased consciousness) or renal impairment limiting clearance. He sits at 2.1 with AKI - imminent dialysis consult is appropriate, particularly with rising level given renal impairment (eTG Toxicology; AMH).

explanation.summary: Lithium has a narrow therapeutic index (target 0.6-0.8 mmol/L maintenance, toxicity often >1.5 mmol/L). NSAID, ACE inhibitor/ARB, thiazide diuretic and dehydration are the four high-yield precipitants. Australian toxicology thresholds support dialysis at level >4.0 mmol/L, or 2.5-4.0 with significant neurotoxicity or impaired renal clearance (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology; AMH).
key_points: Therapeutic range 0.6-0.8 (maintenance); toxicity threshold >1.5 mmol/L
Precipitants: NSAID, ACE-I/ARB, thiazide, dehydration, sodium depletion, AKI
Activated charcoal does NOT bind lithium
Volume repletion with isotonic saline is first-line; avoid loop diuretics
Haemodialysis indicated >4.0 mmol/L, or 2.5-4.0 with neurotoxicity or renal failure
explanation.pearls: Three drug interactions to remember: NSAID + ACE-I + thiazide all increase lithium level by 25-40% (AMH).

stem: A 26-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has had two manic episodes in the past 18 months despite lithium 1000 mg daily (level 0.8 mmol/L). She and her partner are planning a pregnancy within the next 12 months and have stopped using contraception. She is otherwise well, takes no other medications, has normal renal and hepatic function, normal TSH, normal weight, and no history of polycystic ovary syndrome. Her consultant psychiatrist is considering adjuncts to improve mood stability before conception. She asks specifically about sodium valproate, which her sister with epilepsy takes. She has no current depressive symptoms, no psychosis, no current suicide risk, and has been adherent to lithium with stable trough levels.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic mood, no psychosis.
data_table.Investigations: Lithium 0.8 mmol/L, FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH all within reference. Pregnancy test negative.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Add sodium valproate 500 mg BD as adjunct mood stabiliser
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is contraindicated in pregnancy and in women of childbearing potential not on highly effective contraception because of major congenital malformations (~10%) and neurodevelopmental impairment (~30-40%). It must NOT be used unless a Pregnancy Prevention Programme is in place AND no alternative exists. She is actively trying to conceive (TGA black-box; AMH).

options.text: Add quetiapine extended-release 300-600 mg nocte with metabolic monitoring
options.rationale: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG 2020 endorses quetiapine as a first-line option for mood stabilisation in bipolar disorder during pregnancy planning; it has the most reassuring reproductive safety profile among atypical antipsychotics. Add to current lithium with combined metabolic monitoring; this preserves lithium-conferred suicide-prevention effect while improving cycle control (RANZCP; AMH).

options.text: Switch lithium to carbamazepine 200 mg BD as monotherapy
options.rationale: Carbamazepine is also teratogenic (neural tube defects ~1-3%, craniofacial and cardiac malformations) and induces hepatic enzymes including hormonal contraceptives; not first-line in pre-conception planning (AMH).

options.text: Stop lithium immediately because of active pregnancy planning
options.rationale: Stopping lithium pre-conception in a woman with two recent manic episodes carries high relapse risk; current Australian and RANZCP guidance is shared decision-making with risk-benefit, often continuing at lowest effective dose with detailed counselling about Ebstein anomaly risk (low absolute, ~0.05-0.1%) and foetal monitoring (RANZCP perinatal psychiatry; AMH).

options.text: Switch lithium to lamotrigine 200 mg daily as monotherapy
options.rationale: Lamotrigine is effective for depressive maintenance in bipolar but NOT effective for mania prevention; with two recent manic episodes, lamotrigine monotherapy would predictably under-treat (RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate is contraindicated in pregnancy and in any woman of childbearing potential without a Pregnancy Prevention Programme due to major malformation (~10%) and neurodevelopmental impairment (~30-40%) risks. Quetiapine is the first-line alternative in pre-conception planning; lithium can be continued with informed consent; carbamazepine carries neural tube risk; lamotrigine does not prevent mania (RANZCP; TGA).
key_points: Valproate teratogenicity: ~10% major malformations, ~30-40% neurodevelopmental impairment
Australian TGA restriction: avoid in women of childbearing potential without PPP
Quetiapine first-line atypical in bipolar pregnancy planning
Lamotrigine prevents bipolar DEPRESSION not MANIA
Lithium continuation requires foetal cardiac scan at 18-20 weeks
explanation.pearls: If a question mentions both bipolar AND a woman of reproductive age, valproate is almost always the wrong answer (TGA; RANZCP).

subtopic_detail: TCA overdose - cardiac toxicity and sodium bicarbonate
stem: A 32-year-old woman with chronic depression is brought to ED 90 minutes after a deliberate ingestion of approximately 50 amitriptyline 25 mg tablets taken with alcohol. She is drowsy but rousable, GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5). She has had a single self-terminating generalised tonic-clonic seizure on arrival. Her pupils are dilated, mucous membranes dry, urinary catheter has drained 200 mL. ECG shows sinus tachycardia HR 132, QRS 140 ms, terminal R wave in aVR 4 mm, QTc 510 ms. BP is 92/54. Venous blood gas pH 7.28, lactate 3.4, sodium 138, potassium 3.6. No paracetamol co-ingestion detected. She was on no other regular medications.

data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, dilated pupils, dry mucosae, post-ictal.
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus tachycardia, QRS 140 ms, terminal R in aVR 4 mm, QTc 510 ms. VBG: pH 7.28, lactate 3.4, Na 138, K 3.6. Paracetamol level negative.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg bolus, narrow QRS to pH 7.50-7.55
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology criteria for sodium bicarbonate in TCA toxicity: QRS >100 ms, terminal R wave in aVR >3 mm, hypotension, ventricular arrhythmia, or seizures. She meets multiple criteria. Bicarbonate works via sodium loading (overcoming fast sodium channel blockade) AND alkalinisation (reducing free drug). Target QRS narrowing and pH 7.50-7.55 (eTG Toxicology; AMH).

options.text: IV flumazenil 0.2 mg slow push to reverse benzodiazepine sedation
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in mixed overdose where TCA is involved because it can precipitate seizures; she has already seized (eTG Toxicology).

options.text: IV amiodarone 300 mg loading dose for QRS prolongation overnight
options.rationale: Class III antiarrhythmics including amiodarone, and class Ia (procainamide) and Ic agents are CONTRAINDICATED in TCA toxicity because they also block sodium channels and prolong repolarisation, worsening cardiotoxicity (eTG Toxicology; AMH).

options.text: Gastric lavage and oral activated charcoal 50 g for decontamination
options.rationale: Gastric lavage is not recommended after the first hour in most ingestions and has no benefit at 90 minutes; single-dose activated charcoal may be considered within 1-2 hours in an alert patient with protected airway but does not address the life-threatening cardiotoxicity; the primary intervention is sodium bicarbonate (eTG Toxicology).

options.text: IV physostigmine 1 mg slow push to reverse anticholinergic effects
options.rationale: Physostigmine is contraindicated in TCA overdose because it can precipitate asystole, seizures, and worsen cardiotoxicity (eTG Toxicology).

explanation.summary: TCA cardiotoxicity is driven by fast sodium channel blockade producing QRS widening, hypotension, and ventricular arrhythmia. Sodium bicarbonate is the targeted therapy: indications are QRS >100 ms, terminal R in aVR >3 mm, hypotension, ventricular arrhythmia, or seizures. Bolus 1-2 mmol/kg, repeat to narrow QRS and target pH 7.50-7.55 (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).
key_points: Bicarbonate indications: QRS >100 ms, terminal R in aVR >3 mm, hypotension, ventricular arrhythmia, seizures
Contraindicated antiarrhythmics: class Ia, Ic, and III (amiodarone, procainamide, flecainide)
Flumazenil contraindicated in mixed TCA ingestions
Physostigmine contraindicated in TCA overdose
Activated charcoal within 1-2 hours only if alert with protected airway
explanation.pearls: Terminal R wave in aVR >3 mm is the most specific bedside marker of TCA cardiotoxicity (eTG).

stem: A 41-year-old woman who has been on venlafaxine 150 mg daily for 14 months for major depressive disorder agrees with her general practitioner to begin reducing her dose. After her first reduction to 75 mg daily, she develops, within 48 hours: brief electric-shock sensations described as 'brain zaps', dizziness, nausea, headache, insomnia, and intermittent crying spells with irritability. Her PHQ-9 4 days ago at 75 mg was 4. Her mood is reactive, she still finds pleasure in her usual activities, she has no anhedonia, no early morning waking, no suicidal ideation, no diurnal mood variation. Her partner notes she 'feels off' but is still socially engaged and functioning at work. The symptoms began within 48 hours of the dose reduction.

data_table.Examination: Reactive affect, mild irritability, no anhedonia, no psychomotor change, no psychosis.
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 at 150 mg dose 4 days ago: score 4.
data_table.Medications: Venlafaxine 75 mg daily (reduced from 150 mg 48 hours ago).

lead_in: Which is the single most likely cause of her symptoms?

options.text: Recurrence of major depressive disorder
options.rationale: Relapse develops gradually over 2-3 weeks with anhedonia, psychomotor change, neurovegetative features, and progressive functional decline; rapid 48-hour onset of physical neurological symptoms with reactive mood is discontinuation syndrome, not relapse (RANZCP CPG; AMH).

options.text: Serotonin withdrawal-induced GAD
options.rationale: New-onset GAD requires >=6 months of excessive worry; her symptoms began within 48 hours and have a specific neurological pattern fitting discontinuation (DSM-5).

options.text: Serotonin syndrome from drug interaction
options.rationale: Serotonin syndrome features hyperreflexia, clonus, agitation, and autonomic hyperactivity from EXCESS serotonergic activity; she has REDUCED her dose and has no clonus or hyperreflexia (eTG Toxicology).

options.text: Antidepressant discontinuation syndrome
options.rationale: The FINISH mnemonic (Flu-like symptoms, Insomnia, Nausea, Imbalance, Sensory disturbances 'brain zaps', Hyperarousal) captures the syndrome. Onset is within days of dose reduction, lasts 1-2 weeks, and resolves on dose restoration. Venlafaxine has a short half-life (~5 hours; metabolite ~11 hours) and is one of the highest-risk SNRIs for discontinuation symptoms. Distinguishing features from relapse: rapid onset, sensory disturbances, retained mood reactivity, no anhedonia, and resolution with dose restoration (AMH; RANZCP).

options.text: Hypothyroidism from long-term SNRI use
options.rationale: SNRIs do not cause hypothyroidism; brain zaps and rapid onset on dose reduction point to discontinuation syndrome (AMH).

explanation.summary: Discontinuation syndrome (FINISH) develops within days of antidepressant dose reduction, is short-half-life-drug-prone (paroxetine, venlafaxine, duloxetine), lasts 1-2 weeks, and resolves on dose restoration. Relapse develops gradually over weeks with anhedonia and progressive functional decline (AMH; RANZCP).
key_points: FINISH: Flu-like, Insomnia, Nausea, Imbalance, Sensory disturbance (brain zaps), Hyperarousal
Onset within days, resolves within 1-2 weeks or immediately on dose restoration
Highest-risk agents: venlafaxine, paroxetine, duloxetine (short half-life)
Lowest-risk: fluoxetine (long half-life serves as self-taper)
Distinguish from relapse: rapid onset, sensory features, retained reactivity
explanation.pearls: Resolution within 24-48 hours of dose restoration confirms discontinuation rather than relapse (RANZCP).

subtopic_detail: Chronic vs acute risk factors and safety planning
stem: A 38-year-old Aboriginal man presents to ED after an impulsive paracetamol ingestion of 8 tablets following an argument with his partner. He is medically cleared with no transaminitis, paracetamol level below the Rumack-Matthew nomogram line, and ongoing review for psychosocial assessment. He has a history of one previous self-harm episode 4 years ago, a long history of harmful alcohol use (currently 8 standard drinks daily), unemployment for 12 months, and recent eviction. He has chronic back pain and stopped his SSRI 3 months ago because he 'could not afford it'. Tonight he tells you the argument was 'the last straw' and although he says he does not want to die now, he feels 'hopeless about anything getting better'. He has a firearm at home (legally held licensed pest control firearm). He has a brother who completed suicide 8 years ago. He has not yet been linked with the local Aboriginal Community Controlled Health Service.

data_table.Examination: Hopeless, mildly intoxicated, no psychosis, no agitation, no suicide plan at this moment.
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol level non-toxic. LFTs normal. UEC normal. BAC 0.18%.

lead_in: Which is the single most important MODIFIABLE acute risk factor to address before discharge planning?

options.text: Brother's completed suicide from eight years ago
options.rationale: Family history of suicide is a non-modifiable chronic risk factor; while important to acknowledge it cannot be changed and is not the priority intervention target tonight (RANZCP suicide risk).
source_refs: RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk

options.text: His Aboriginal identity as a demographic factor
options.rationale: Aboriginal identity is NOT a risk factor; structural disadvantage, intergenerational trauma, and reduced access to culturally safe care are. Linking with the Aboriginal Community Controlled Health Service is an important protective intervention, but framing identity itself as a risk factor is wrong (RANZCP; Gayaa Dhuwi).
source_refs: RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk

options.text: Previous self-harm episode from four years ago
options.rationale: Past self-harm is a strong chronic risk factor but non-modifiable historically; it informs risk stratification but is not the modifiable intervention target (RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk

options.text: Pervasive hopelessness about the future improving
options.rationale: Hopelessness is a modifiable cognitive factor addressed by psychotherapy, social support, and treatment of underlying depression but acts over weeks; means restriction acts immediately and prevents impulsive action in the highest-risk 48-72 hours post-presentation (RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk

options.text: Access to a firearm at home
options.rationale: Means restriction is the single highest-impact MODIFIABLE acute intervention in suicide prevention. Firearm access in Australia approximately triples completed suicide risk and is the most lethal method; safely removing the firearm before discharge (police-held storage, family-held storage, voluntary surrender) is the priority risk-reduction action. RANZCP and Australian suicide prevention guidance both centre means restriction as the most evidence-based modifiable acute factor (RANZCP; LIFE Framework).
source_refs: RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk
Australian Government LIFE: Living Is For Everyone Framework

explanation.summary: Suicide risk is structured into static (chronic, non-modifiable: previous attempt, family history, male sex, mental illness) and dynamic (acute, modifiable: hopelessness, recent stressors, intoxication, means access, lack of social connection). Means restriction is the single most evidence-based modifiable acute factor in Australian and international guidelines (RANZCP; LIFE Framework).
key_points: Chronic non-modifiable: prior attempt, family history of suicide, male sex, chronic illness, prior trauma
Acute modifiable: means access, intoxication, recent stressors, hopelessness, lack of supports
Means restriction = highest-impact acute modifiable intervention
Safety plan: warning signs, internal coping, social distractors, supports, professional help, means restriction
Aboriginal mental health care: link with ACCHS for culturally safe follow-up
explanation.pearls: If a question lists access to a firearm or large medication stockpile, that is the modifiable factor to prioritise (RANZCP).

sources.label: RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk
Australian Government LIFE: Living Is For Everyone Framework

subtopic_detail: Safety plan after self-harm (DBT chain analysis)
stem: A 23-year-old woman with borderline personality disorder presents to ED after superficial wrist cutting following a perceived rejection by a friend. She has had 6 similar presentations over the past 12 months. She is medically cleared after wound closure and tetanus update. She has an established treatment relationship with a community DBT-informed team. She declines admission and her case manager is contactable. She is no longer acutely distressed and denies suicidal intent. She agrees that she 'just snapped' when she saw the unread message. She asks for help with what to do next time. She has had no alcohol or substances tonight. She lives with her mother who is supportive and present in ED.

data_table.Examination: Calm, reactive affect, no current suicidal ideation, no psychosis, superficial laceration sutured.
data_table.Investigations: Wounds superficial, tetanus updated, paracetamol level negative, BAC zero.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step before discharge?

options.text: Admit involuntarily under the Mental Health Act for inpatient stabilisation
options.rationale: Involuntary admission requires that the person is suffering a mental illness AND there is no less restrictive option appropriate to their care; she has capacity, no active suicidal intent, available community supports, and an established outpatient team. Involuntary admission for repeated low-lethality self-harm in BPD is often counter-therapeutic and is not standard practice (RANZCP BPD CPG).

options.text: Discharge with a written 'no-suicide contract' signed by the patient
options.rationale: No-suicide contracts have no evidence of efficacy, may reduce clinical vigilance, and are not endorsed by RANZCP or international guidance (RANZCP suicide).
source_refs: RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk

options.text: Collaboratively develop a Stanley-Brown safety plan and contact her DBT team
options.rationale: The Stanley-Brown Safety Planning Intervention is the Australian-endorsed structured approach: warning signs, internal coping strategies, social contacts and settings for distraction, supportive people to contact, professionals/agencies, and means restriction. Pairing this with handover to the established DBT team allows chain analysis at next session to identify triggers, vulnerabilities, and skill alternatives. Evidence supports SPI reducing repeat suicidal behaviour by ~50% at 6 months (RANZCP; LIFE Framework; Stanley-Brown).
source_refs: RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk
Australian Government LIFE: Living Is For Everyone Framework

options.text: Commence olanzapine 10 mg daily for affective instability and agitation
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is not first-line for BPD and antipsychotics have only limited evidence for some symptom domains; introducing medication in ED without longitudinal review is not endorsed (RANZCP BPD CPG).

options.text: Refer to drug and alcohol service for substance-use treatment
options.rationale: She has no alcohol or substance use tonight or per history in the stem; not the priority (RANZCP).

explanation.summary: After a self-harm presentation, collaborative safety planning (Stanley-Brown) combined with continuity of psychological care is evidence-based and reduces repeat suicidal behaviour. DBT chain analysis at the next session unpacks the precipitating chain. Involuntary admission, no-suicide contracts, and ED-initiated pharmacotherapy are not first-line (RANZCP; LIFE Framework).
key_points: Stanley-Brown Safety Plan: 6 components, collaboratively developed, written copy with patient
Continuity > containment: handover to established team is critical
No-suicide contracts have no evidence and may be harmful
BPD first-line treatment is structured psychotherapy (DBT, MBT, schema)
Chain analysis identifies vulnerabilities, precipitants, behaviour, consequences for skills practice
explanation.pearls: If a question describes superficial self-harm in a person with BPD and intact supports, safety plan + community handover beats admission (RANZCP).

sources.label: RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk
Australian Government LIFE: Living Is For Everyone Framework

stem: A 68-year-old man is reviewed in stroke clinic 8 weeks after a moderate left middle cerebral artery ischaemic stroke. He has residual mild right-sided weakness and mild expressive dysphasia from which he is recovering. His wife reports that since week 3, he has been progressively withdrawn, has stopped attending physiotherapy sessions, cries during morning routines, has lost 4 kg, sleeps 9 hours but wakes unrefreshed, and says repeatedly 'I'm a burden'. His PHQ-9 today is 17 (excluding his neurological items). He has no prior psychiatric history. His current medications are aspirin 100 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg, perindopril 5 mg, and a multivitamin. He has no suicidal plan but admits passive thoughts that 'it would be easier if I didn't wake up'. There is no evidence of vascular depression alone explaining the pattern, and no apathy syndrome without mood symptoms.

data_table.Examination: Mild right hemiparesis (improving), mild expressive dysphasia, low affect, congruent, no psychosis.
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 17. FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH, B12, folate all within reference.
data_table.Medications: Aspirin 100 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, perindopril 5 mg, multivitamin.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Sertraline 25 mg daily titrated to 50 mg, plus structured psychotherapy and rehabilitation engagement
options.rationale: Post-stroke depression affects approximately 1 in 3 stroke survivors, peaks at 3-6 months, and meaningfully impairs rehabilitation outcomes. SSRI treatment (sertraline or citalopram are the preferred Australian first-line options for cardiovascular comorbidity) plus active rehabilitation engagement improves both mood and functional outcome. Combination beats either modality alone (Stroke Foundation Australia Clinical Guidelines; RANZCP).
source_refs: Stroke Foundation Australia: Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management

options.text: Reassure that low mood is normal after stroke and review in 3 months
options.rationale: PHQ-9 of 17 reflects moderate-to-severe depression with functional impact (refusing rehabilitation, passive death thoughts); delaying treatment by 3 months risks worse functional outcomes and is not consistent with Stroke Foundation guidance (Stroke Foundation).
source_refs: Stroke Foundation Australia: Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management

options.text: Start mirtazapine 30 mg nocte plus low-dose amitriptyline 25 mg for sleep and mood
options.rationale: Amitriptyline (TCA) has significant anticholinergic, orthostatic, and cardiac risks in older post-stroke patients and is not first-line; sertraline or citalopram is preferred (AMH).

options.text: Refer urgently for ECT as first-line monotherapy
options.rationale: ECT is reserved for severe depression with catatonia, food/fluid refusal, high suicide risk, or treatment resistance; not first-line in moderate post-stroke depression (RANZCP).

options.text: Add diazepam 5 mg BD orally for anxiety symptoms
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines in older adults increase falls, cognitive impairment, and second stroke risk; not appropriate for this mood presentation (AMH).

explanation.summary: Post-stroke depression affects ~30% of stroke survivors, peaks at 3-6 months, and worsens rehabilitation outcomes. Stroke Foundation Australia endorses SSRI plus active rehabilitation engagement as first-line. Sertraline or citalopram are preferred due to cardiovascular safety profile (Stroke Foundation; RANZCP).
key_points: PSD prevalence ~30%, peak 3-6 months
Australian first-line SSRI: sertraline or citalopram
Avoid TCAs in older post-stroke patients
Combine pharmacotherapy with active rehabilitation engagement
PHQ-9 may overestimate due to overlap with stroke-related items - interpret carefully
explanation.pearls: Untreated PSD worsens not just mood but motor and cognitive recovery (Stroke Foundation Australia).

sources.label: Stroke Foundation Australia: Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management

subtopic_detail: Postpartum mood disorder after pregnancy loss
stem: A 31-year-old woman attends her general practitioner 6 weeks after a stillbirth at 32 weeks gestation. She reports persistent low mood, anhedonia, insomnia with early morning waking, weight loss of 3 kg, feelings of worthlessness, and intermittent passive thoughts of being better off not being here. She has not returned to work and avoids contact with friends who have babies. She has no psychotic symptoms, no delusions or hallucinations. Her Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) today is 18 with item 10 (self-harm) scored 1. She has had no prior psychiatric history. She is not breastfeeding. She would like to try for another pregnancy in the future. She lives with a supportive partner.

data_table.Examination: Tearful, congruent affect, no psychosis, no thought disorder.
data_table.Investigations: EPDS 18, item 10 score 1. TSH 1.6, FBC, UEC, LFTs, B12, ferritin all within reference.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Watchful waiting for grief resolution over 3-6 months with no treatment
options.rationale: EPDS of 18 with neurovegetative symptoms, functional impairment, and passive death thoughts at 6 weeks post-loss meets criteria for major depressive disorder, NOT uncomplicated grief; expectant management is inappropriate (RANZCP perinatal; COPE).
source_refs: COPE: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period - Australian Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Diagnose prolonged grief disorder and offer structured grief therapy alone
options.rationale: Prolonged grief disorder DSM-5-TR requires symptoms beyond 12 months in adults; at 6 weeks, comorbid MDD is the diagnosis with priority intervention (DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Start a benzodiazepine for short-term anxiolysis and review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not first-line for depressive disorders, carry dependence risk, and would be considered only briefly if at all for severe anxiety; they do not treat the depressive presentation (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Sertraline 25-50 mg daily plus structured psychotherapy with perinatal-experienced clinician
options.rationale: COPE Perinatal Guideline 2023 recommends combined pharmacotherapy plus structured psychotherapy for moderate-to-severe perinatal depression. Sertraline is a first-line Australian SSRI in perinatal contexts including breastfeeding (not relevant here) and pregnancy planning. SANDS and Red Nose provide bereavement-specific peer support after stillbirth or neonatal loss. Combining biological treatment with grief-informed psychological care is the evidence-based standard (COPE; RANZCP).
source_refs: COPE: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period - Australian Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Recommend immediate next pregnancy as therapeutic to resolve mood symptoms
options.rationale: There is no evidence that next pregnancy treats perinatal depression and it may worsen outcomes; depression should be treated before conception planning (COPE).
source_refs: COPE: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period - Australian Clinical Practice Guideline

explanation.summary: Perinatal mental health screening (EPDS, score >=13 prompts assessment) after pregnancy loss identifies treatable depression that should not be dismissed as 'normal grief'. COPE 2023 endorses combined SSRI plus structured psychotherapy with grief-informed peer support. Sertraline is the preferred Australian SSRI in perinatal contexts (COPE; RANZCP).
key_points: EPDS >=13 = positive screen; item 10 always reviewed for self-harm
Grief vs depression: grief is undulating; depression is pervasive with worthlessness and neurovegetative features
Sertraline is first-line Australian perinatal SSRI
SANDS and Red Nose are Australian bereavement supports
Prolonged grief disorder requires >=12 months in adults
explanation.pearls: After stillbirth, ask about EVERY pregnancy loss in subsequent years - anniversary reactions are common (COPE).

sources.label: COPE: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period - Australian Clinical Practice Guideline

subtopic_detail: Adolescent suicide risk - specific risk factors
stem: A 16-year-old young person is brought to ED by their mother after she found drafts of a goodbye note on their bedroom desk. The young person reluctantly discloses they have been experiencing low mood for 4 months, having previously been a strong student now failing assessments. HEEADSSS interview reveals: school refusal beginning when they came out as transgender to peers and experienced 6 weeks of escalating online abuse, current alcohol use 2-3 nights per week with peers, no current medications, broken sleep, and a friend who completed suicide 5 months ago. They feel rejected by their father since coming out. Their mother is supportive but exhausted. They acknowledge a specific plan involving paracetamol stockpiled at home, with intended timing this weekend when their mother is travelling. There is no current psychosis. They have no current GP and are not engaged with any mental health service.

data_table.Examination: Withdrawn, restricted affect, no psychosis, fully oriented.
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol level negative on arrival. UEC, LFTs, FBC within reference.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with mother, written safety plan, and outpatient youth mental health referral
options.rationale: Specific plan, available means at home, intended timing within 48 hours when supervision will be unavailable, recent bereavement by suicide, escalating bullying, gender minority stress, and substance use combine to indicate HIGH acute risk that cannot be safely managed in the home with current supports tonight (headspace; RANZCP youth suicide).
source_refs: headspace: youth mental health clinical guidance
RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk

options.text: Admit (voluntary or involuntary) to adolescent mental health unit and link with LGBTQAI+ youth supports
options.rationale: Multiple acute high-risk features (specific plan + means + timing + recent peer suicide + active bullying + alienation from one parent + substance use + lack of professional supports) require an inpatient level of safety while initial assessment, family work, and treatment are established. Australian Mental Health Acts permit involuntary admission of minors when criteria are met. Affirming the young person's identity and connecting with LGBTQAI+ youth services (QLife, Twenty10, Minus18) is integral to care - gender minority youth suicide risk is elevated by family rejection and reduced by family acceptance, school connectedness, and culturally safe care (headspace; RANZCP; Trans Pathways).
source_refs: headspace: youth mental health clinical guidance
RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk

options.text: Commence fluoxetine 10 mg daily tonight in ED and discharge home with parent
options.rationale: Fluoxetine is first-line for adolescent MDD where indicated, but starting in ED with discharge into a high acute-risk environment does not address the immediate safety problem; medication takes weeks to act (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Notify child protection services and remove the young person from the home
options.rationale: Mandatory reporting applies where there is significant risk of harm from a parent; the mother is supportive and the immediate risk is to self, not from parental abuse. Inpatient admission addresses the immediate safety problem; family work follows (Children and Young Persons Act state-specific guidance).
source_refs: headspace: youth mental health clinical guidance

options.text: Reassure as 'normal teenage stress' and arrange routine GP follow-up next week
options.rationale: This pattern - specific plan, means, timing, recent peer suicide, active bullying - is one of the highest-acuity adolescent presentations; minimising is dangerous and not consistent with any Australian guidance (RANZCP; headspace).
source_refs: RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk
headspace: youth mental health clinical guidance

explanation.summary: Adolescent-specific suicide risk factors include peer suicide bereavement (contagion), bullying (especially cyberbullying), gender and sexuality minority stress, family rejection, substance use, and impulsivity. The Australian Trans Pathways study documented dramatically elevated self-harm in transgender youth, largely modifiable by family acceptance, peer connectedness, and access to gender-affirming care. Multiple acute features mandate inpatient safety in this presentation (headspace; RANZCP; Trans Pathways).
key_points: HEEADSSS structures adolescent psychosocial screening
Adolescent-specific risks: peer suicide, bullying, identity-related stress, family rejection
LGBTQAI+ supports: QLife (1800 184 527), Twenty10, Minus18, headspace
Family acceptance is a strong protective factor for transgender youth
Mental Health Acts permit involuntary admission of minors when criteria met
explanation.pearls: Means restriction at home + LGBTQAI+ affirming care are two of the highest-yield modifiable interventions in transgender youth suicide prevention (Trans Pathways).

sources.label: headspace: youth mental health clinical guidance
RANZCP: Assessment and management of suicide risk

subtopic_detail: B12 deficiency presenting as cognitive and mood symptoms
stem: A 72-year-old woman is referred to community psychiatry by her general practitioner with a 5-month history of progressive low mood, irritability, paranoid ideas about her neighbour, episodes of confusion, and worsening short-term memory. She has lost 6 kg, has reduced appetite, and complains of intermittent paraesthesia in both feet and unsteady gait, particularly in the dark. She has fallen twice in 3 months. Past medical history includes autoimmune thyroiditis on stable levothyroxine (TSH 1.8 today) and 12 months of pantoprazole 40 mg daily for reflux. She has been a strict vegan for 25 years and takes no vitamin supplements. She drinks no alcohol. On examination she has glossitis with a beefy tongue, mild distal sensory loss to vibration and proprioception in both feet, brisk knee reflexes with absent ankle reflexes, Romberg positive, and MoCA 19 of 30. Skin is pale with a lemon-yellow tint. FBC and film: Hb 95 g/L, MCV 112 fL, hypersegmented neutrophils on film.

data_table.Examination: Glossitis, lemon-yellow pallor, reduced vibration and proprioception in both feet, brisk knee reflexes, absent ankle reflexes, Romberg positive, MoCA 19/30.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 95 g/L (low), MCV 112 fL (high), hypersegmented neutrophils on film. TSH 1.8. UEC normal.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Serum B12, folate, ferritin, methylmalonic acid, homocysteine, and intrinsic factor antibodies
options.rationale: Macrocytic anaemia (MCV 112) with hypersegmented neutrophils, glossitis, dorsal column signs (vibration/proprioception loss, Romberg positive), brisk knee + absent ankle reflexes (subacute combined degeneration pattern), cognitive impairment, mood and paranoid features in a long-term vegan ALSO on chronic PPI therapy is a textbook B12 deficiency. Australian first-line workup is serum B12 + folate; methylmalonic acid and homocysteine confirm intracellular deficiency when B12 is borderline. Intrinsic factor antibodies identify pernicious anaemia. Hypothyroidism is already excluded by current TSH (Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology; AMH).
source_refs: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview

options.text: Brain MRI for suspected vascular dementia and silent infarcts
options.rationale: Vascular dementia can cause cognitive and mood symptoms but does NOT cause macrocytic anaemia, glossitis, hypersegmented neutrophils, or selective dorsal column dysfunction; the haematological and neurological pattern points to B12 deficiency, not vascular pathology (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).

options.text: Lumbar puncture for autoimmune encephalitis antibody workup
options.rationale: Autoimmune encephalitis typically presents sub-acutely with psychiatric symptoms plus seizures, movement disorder, and autonomic instability; her macrocytic anaemia and dorsal column signs do not fit (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).

options.text: Repeat TSH and free T4 to rule out hypothyroid relapse
options.rationale: TSH today is 1.8 within reference; hypothyroidism does not produce macrocytosis of this degree (MCV 112), hypersegmented neutrophils, glossitis, or selective dorsal column signs (AMH).

options.text: CT abdomen for occult malignancy as cause of weight loss
options.rationale: Weight loss has a plausible nutritional explanation (long-term veganism, B12 deficiency, glossitis impairing eating); occult malignancy workup is reasonable later but is not the immediate next step given a clear B12 deficiency pattern (Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology).

explanation.summary: B12 deficiency is a high-yield psychiatric mimic in older adults, vegans, and those on chronic acid suppression. The diagnostic fingerprint integrates macrocytic anaemia + hypersegmented neutrophils + glossitis + selective dorsal column signs (subacute combined degeneration) + cognitive and mood symptoms. Australian workup pairs B12 with folate, ferritin, and confirmatory MMA/homocysteine; intrinsic factor antibodies define pernicious anaemia (Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology; AMH).
key_points: High-risk groups: older adults, vegans, chronic PPI/H2RA, metformin, post-gastrectomy/bariatric, ileal disease
Subacute combined degeneration: dorsal columns (vibration, proprioception) + corticospinal tracts (brisk knee, absent ankle reflexes, Babinski)
Workup: B12, folate, ferritin; MMA and homocysteine if borderline; intrinsic factor antibodies for PA
Replacement: IM hydroxocobalamin 1 mg alternate days for 2 weeks then 3-monthly (PA) or as required
Mood, psychosis, and cognitive symptoms can be the presenting feature - check B12 in any new geriatric mood or cognitive presentation
explanation.pearls: Brisk knee reflex WITH absent ankle reflex is the classic mixed-tract sign of subacute combined degeneration (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).

sources.label: RANZCP: Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders overview
